unverified_claims.note: The 75,000 monthly search volume is a directional estimate for combined searches on 'precision agriculture', 'smart farming', 'AI farming', and related terms. No primary keyword tool (Keywords Everywhere, Ahrefs, SEMrush) was directly queried. The estimate is based on the compound interest of adjacent search terms. 'Precision agriculture' alone is estimated at 40K-60K globally by industry SEO practitioners. The figure may be higher when including regional language variants and broader agtech queries.

The +159% five-year growth is calculated from an estimated AI in agriculture market baseline of ~$1.2B in 2021 growing to $3.11B in 2026 at the documented 21.96% CAGR (Mordor Intelligence). The 2021 baseline is an estimate — no single authoritative source provided a precise 2021 AI in agriculture market figure. The calculation: $1.2B * (1.2196)^5 = $3.11B → +159%. Different analysts use different market definitions (AI in ag vs. digital farming vs. precision ag) producing different baseline figures; the trend direction is consistent across all sources.

The +14% YoY figure is based on US agrifoodtech investment growth (AgFunder News: $345M in 2024 vs $303M estimated run-rate 2023) — a full-year investment metric, not a true Q-over-Q three-month sequential growth figure. No published Q1 2026 agricultural technology revenue or market growth report was found. The figure is used as the best available documented proxy for recent-quarter market momentum. Actual 3-month sequential market growth may differ.

unverified_claims.claim: Economic impact: ~$500M+ in farmer input savings from See & Spray (2025)
unverified_claims.note: The $500M+ savings estimate is calculated from John Deere's documented 31M gallons saved multiplied by an average commercial herbicide cost of ~$16-20 per gallon. John Deere's source confirms 31M gallons saved (AgTech Navigator, Nov 2025); the cost per gallon is a range estimate. Actual dollar savings depend on herbicide type, concentration, and contract pricing which vary by crop, region, and customer.

unverified_claims.claim: 84% of world's farms are under 2 hectares
unverified_claims.note: This statistic is widely cited in development economics and agtech literature and is attributed to FAO's 2014 Census of Agriculture. The FAO figure may have shifted somewhat since 2014 given structural changes in landholding patterns, particularly in Asia and Africa. The directional truth — that the vast majority of the world's farms are smallholder operations largely unserved by current AI tools — is well-supported across multiple authoritative sources.
